full_response:
I'll solve this step-by-step to show that 1g is approximately equal to 1 lightyear/year².

## Step 1: Convert 1g to SI units

Standard gravitational acceleration:
$$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

## Step 2: Convert to lightyear/year² units

I need to express this acceleration in terms of lightyears per year squared.

First, let me establish the conversion factors:
- 1 lightyear = distance light travels in 1 year
- Speed of light: $c = 3.00 \times 10^8$ m/s
- 1 year = 365.25 days × 24 hours × 3600 seconds = $3.156 \times 10^7$ s

Distance of 1 lightyear:
$$1 \text{ ly} = c \times 1 \text{ year} = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \times 3.156 \times 10^7 \text{ s} = 9.468 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}$$

## Step 3: Convert 1g to lightyear/year²

$$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$

To convert m/s² to ly/year², I need:
- Convert meters to lightyears: divide by $9.468 \times 10^{15}$
- Convert seconds² to years²: multiply by $(3.156 \times 10^7)^2$

$$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times \frac{1 \text{ ly}}{9.468 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}} \times \frac{(3.156 \times 10^7 \text{ s})^2}{1 \text{ year}^2}$$

$$g = 9.81 \times \frac{1}{9.468 \times 10^{15}} \times (3.156 \times 10^7)^2 \text{ ly/year}^2$$

$$g = 9.81 \times \frac{9.960 \times 10^{14}}{9.468 \times 10^{15}} \text{ ly/year}^2$$

$$g = 9.81 \times 0.1052 \text{ ly/year}^2$$

$$g = 1.032 \text{ ly/year}^2$$

## Step 4: Verification using c = 1 units

In natural units where c = 1, we have:
- 1 lightyear = 1 year × c = 1 year (in distance units)
- So 1 ly/year² = c/year = 1/year

The proper conversion is:
$$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times \frac{1 \text{ year}}{3.156 \times 10^7 \text{ s}} \times \frac{1 \text{ ly}}{9.468 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}} \times \text{year}^2$$

This gives the same result: $g \approx 1.03$ ly/year².

## Step 5: Conclusion

The calculation shows that:
$$1g = 1.032 \text{ ly/year}^2 \approx 1.0 \text{ ly/year}^2$$

This is indeed very close to 1 lightyear/year², with only about 3% difference. The approximation $g \approx 1$ ly/year² is excellent for relativistic problems involving interstellar distances and time scales.

\boxed{1g = 1.032 \text{ ly/year}^2 \approx 1.0 \text{ ly/year}^2, confirming that 1g is very close to 1 lightyear/year²}

Confidence: 95%
extracted_answer: 1g = 1.032 \text{ ly/year}^2 \approx 1.0 \text{ ly/year}^2, confirming that 1g is very close to 1 lightyear/year²
